d2a632c4-6a46-43e6-b4a6-373d2793084b.titulo: Electrical quantities: voltage, current, resistance, power and energy
d2a632c4-6a46-43e6-b4a6-373d2793084b.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Before measuring, soldering or connecting any equipment, you need to master the language of electricity. This is the alphabet of the entire course.

All electronics rests on a few fundamental quantities. Understanding them precisely — including correct units and symbols — avoids costly mistakes and dangerous situations on stage and in the rack.

  • Electric charge (Q): A fundamental property of matter, measured in coulombs (C). 1 C is equivalent to the charge of approximately 6.24×10¹⁸ electrons. Current is nothing more than charge in motion.
  • Voltage (V): Also called potential difference or p.d. It is the 'push' that moves the electrons, measured in volts (V). One volt corresponds to one joule per coulomb (1 V = 1 J/C). It is always measured BETWEEN two points. Unit symbol: V; quantity symbol: U or V.
  • Electric current (I): The ordered flow of charges, measured in amperes (A). 1 A = 1 coulomb per second (1 C/s). By convention, conventional current flows from + to − (opposite to the actual movement of the electrons). It is measured in series with the circuit.
  • Resistance (R): The opposition to the passage of current, measured in ohms (Ω). It depends on the material, length, cross-section and temperature of the conductor. Its inverse is conductance, measured in siemens (S).
  • Power (P): The rate of energy transfer, measured in watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. In electricity, P = V×I. It is what defines the consumption of a piece of equipment and the sizing of power supplies and cables.
  • Energy (E): Power over time, measured in joules (J) or, on the electricity bill, in kilowatt-hours (kWh). 1 kWh = 3.6×10⁶ J. It is energy, not power, that you pay the utility company for.
  • Prefixes and notation: Master the multiples and submultiples: milli (m, 10⁻³), micro (µ, 10⁻⁶), nano (n, 10⁻⁹), pico (p, 10⁻¹²), kilo (k, 10³), mega (M, 10⁶), giga (G, 10⁹). Confusing mV with V or µF with mF is a classic source of error.

Equipment / details

  • Multimeter: Measures voltage, current and resistance; the basic instrument for checking all of these quantities.
  • Wattmeter/energy meter: Measures the power and consumption (kWh) of event equipment.

Step by step

  1. Identify the quantity: Before measuring, determine whether you are looking for voltage (between two points), current (in the path) or resistance (with the circuit de-energized).
  2. Check the unit and prefix: Read the display paying attention to mV/V and mA/A; a factor of 1000 completely changes the interpretation.
  3. Relate it to power: Multiply the measured voltage by the current to estimate the power consumed by the equipment.

In practice: In the rack of a show, an operator sees '0.5' on the multimeter and thinks they measured 0.5 V, but the instrument was set to mV — it was 0.5 mV, negligible noise. Reading the unit and prefix correctly is as important as the number.

Summary

  • Voltage (V), current (A) and resistance (Ω) are the three basic quantities; power (W) is V×I.
  • Energy is power over time, paid in kWh, not in watts.
  • Mastering prefixes (m, µ, k, M) avoids factor-of-1000 errors in measurements.

fa02a582-0882-45b0-8daa-72eed63a22dd.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Three equations govern practically every circuit you will analyze in your career. Let's master them for good.

Ohm's Law relates voltage, current and resistance; Kirchhoff's Laws describe how voltages and currents are distributed in any network. Together, they are the foundation of circuit calculation.

  • Ohm's Law: V = R×I. The voltage across a resistor is the product of the resistance and the current flowing through it. From it derive I = V/R and R = V/I. It holds for ohmic components (resistors), in which R is constant.
  • Dissipated power: Combining with P = V×I, we obtain P = I²×R and P = V²/R. These are essential formulas for sizing the power (wattage) of a resistor and preventing it from burning out.
  • Kirchhoff's 1st Law (Currents / KCL): The sum of the currents entering a node equals the sum of those leaving it. It is the conservation of charge: nothing accumulates at the node. ΣI_in = ΣI_out.
  • Kirchhoff's 2nd Law (Voltages / KVL): The algebraic sum of the voltages in any closed loop is zero. Rising through sources and dropping across loads, once you go all the way around you return to the same potential. ΣV = 0.
  • Sign convention: Define a current direction, mark polarities (+/−) on the components and be consistent. A negative result only indicates that the actual direction is opposite to the assumed one.
  • Ohmic vs. non-ohmic materials: Resistors follow Ohm's Law (the V×I graph is a straight line). Diodes, lamps and semiconductors are non-ohmic: the V×I relationship is a curve.

Equipment / details

  • Multimeter: Checks voltage and current to validate Ohm and Kirchhoff calculations.
  • Bench power supply: Applies a controlled voltage to study the V×I relationship of a component.

Step by step

  1. Draw the circuit: Identify nodes, loops, sources and resistors before calculating.
  2. Assume directions: Choose current directions in each branch and polarities on the components.
  3. Apply KCL and KVL: Write one current equation per node and one voltage equation per independent loop.
  4. Solve and check: Solve the system; negative signs indicate a reversed direction. Verify by adding up the powers.

In practice: To light a 2 V, 20 mA LED from 12 V, the series resistor is calculated using Ohm's Law: R = (12−2)/0.02 = 500 Ω. The power in it is P = I²R = 0.02²×500 = 0.2 W, so a 1/4 W resistor is already at the limit — a 1/2 W one is used for safety.

Summary

  • Ohm's Law: V = R×I, with derivatives for current and resistance.
  • Kirchhoff: currents are conserved at the node (KCL) and voltages sum to zero in the loop (KVL).
  • Power via P=I²R and P=V²/R sizes the wattage of the components.

d2c25d96-c155-4f52-80bb-2d95b00abb23.titulo: DC vs AC, series and parallel circuits
d2c25d96-c155-4f52-80bb-2d95b00abb23.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The walkie's battery and the stage outlet deliver energy in radically different ways. Understanding DC, AC and how components are combined is decisive.

Direct and alternating current behave differently; and the way resistors are combined (in series or parallel) changes voltages and currents throughout the circuit.

  • Direct current (DC): Constant polarity and direction over time. Supplied by batteries, cells and power supplies. Ideal for low-voltage electronics (5 V, 12 V, 48 V).
  • Alternating current (AC): Direction and magnitude vary periodically, generally sinusoidally. The Brazilian power grid operates at 60 Hz. It allows efficient transmission and the use of transformers.
  • AC values: Distinguish peak value (Vp), peak-to-peak (Vpp) and effective/RMS (Vrms). For a sine wave, Vrms = Vp/√2. The 127 V or 220 V from the outlet are RMS values.
  • Frequency and period: Frequency (f) in hertz (Hz) is the number of cycles per second; period T = 1/f. At 60 Hz, T ≈ 16.7 ms.
  • Series combination: Components in sequence share the SAME current. Resistances add up: Req = R1+R2+... The voltage divides in proportion to each resistance.
  • Parallel combination: Components connected between the same two nodes share the SAME voltage. Conductances add up: 1/Req = 1/R1+1/R2+... The current divides. The Req is always smaller than the smallest resistor.
  • Case of two in parallel: For just two resistors: Req = (R1×R2)/(R1+R2), the famous 'product over sum'. For N equal resistors R, Req = R/N.

Equipment / details

  • Multimeter: Measures DC/AC voltage (RMS) and the equivalent resistance of combinations.
  • Oscilloscope: Shows the AC waveform, peak, peak-to-peak and frequency.

Step by step

  1. Classify the source: Check whether it is DC (battery/power supply) or AC (grid) before measuring.
  2. Select the correct function: On the multimeter, choose V⎓ (DC) or V∼ (AC) according to the source.
  3. Identify series or parallel: Same current = series; same voltage (same two nodes) = parallel.
  4. Calculate the equivalent: Add resistances in series; use product-over-sum or the inverse of the sums in parallel.

In practice: Two 8 Ω speakers connected in parallel on the same channel result in 4 Ω, demanding twice the current from the amplifier. Ignoring this can overload it and trip/shut down the amp — which is why speaker impedance is a critical matter.

Summary

  • DC has fixed polarity; AC alternates periodically (grid at 60 Hz).
  • The outlet voltage is an RMS value; for a sine wave Vrms = Vp/√2.
  • Series adds resistances and divides voltage; parallel divides current and reduces resistance.

80a03a55-131c-489f-b917-eff8963b4793.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Two simple circuits appear in nearly every project: the voltage divider and the current divider. Let's calculate both with confidence.

Dividers allow you to obtain fractions of a voltage or current — the basis of sensors, biasing and level adjustments.

  • Voltage divider: With two resistors in series, the voltage across R2 is Vout = Vin × R2/(R1+R2). Used to reduce voltages, bias transistor bases and create references.
  • Loading effect: If a load is connected to the output, it ends up in parallel with R2 and alters the ratio. For a stable divider, the load current must be much smaller than the divider current.
  • Current divider: With two resistors in parallel, the current in one branch is a fraction of the total. For R1 and R2: I1 = Itotal × R2/(R1+R2). Current prefers the path of least resistance.
  • Potentiometer as a divider: A potentiometer is an adjustable voltage divider: the wiper delivers a continuous fraction of the voltage applied across the ends. It is the basis of faders and volume knobs.
  • Application in sensors: Resistive sensors (NTC, LDR) form a divider with a fixed resistor; the output voltage varies with temperature or light, being read by an ADC.

Equipment / details

  • Resistors and potentiometer: Build fixed and adjustable dividers.
  • Multimeter: Measures the output voltage of the divider to validate the calculation.

Step by step

  1. Define Vin and the desired fraction: Determine the input voltage and the target Vout.
  2. Choose R1 and R2: Calculate the ratio R2/(R1+R2) to obtain the desired Vout.
  3. Check the divider current: Ensure enough current so that the load does not pull the output down.
  4. Measure and adjust: Check Vout with the multimeter with the load already connected.

In practice: To read a volume fader on a digital mixer, 3.3 V is applied across the ends of the potentiometer; the wiper delivers 0 to 3.3 V to the ADC, which converts the position into a gain value. It is a pure variable voltage divider.

Summary

  • Voltage divider: Vout = Vin×R2/(R1+R2), the basis of biasing and references.
  • A load in parallel with R2 alters the output; keep the divider current high.
  • The current divider splits the current; it prefers the smaller resistor.

6aac751f-27db-4c41-ac9e-fd9f40c95828.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The three fundamental passive components. Whoever understands the resistor, capacitor and inductor understands half of electronics.

Resistors dissipate, capacitors store energy in an electric field and inductors in a magnetic field. Each reacts differently to DC and AC.

  • Resistor: Limits current and dissipates energy as heat. Characterized by resistance (Ω), power (W) and tolerance (%). Types: carbon film, metal film (more precise), wirewound (high power), SMD.
  • Color code: Axial resistors use colored bands: the first two/three are digits, the next one is the multiplier and the last is the tolerance (gold ±5%, silver ±10%). Master the sequence black-brown-red-orange-yellow-green-blue-violet-gray-white (0 to 9).
  • Capacitor: Stores charge in an electric field between plates; capacitance in farads (F), in practice µF, nF, pF. It blocks DC in steady state and lets AC through. Types: ceramic, polyester, electrolytic (polarized!), tantalum.
  • Capacitive reactance: The capacitor's opposition to AC: Xc = 1/(2πfC), in ohms. The higher the frequency, the lower the opposition — which is why capacitors couple signals and filter.
  • Inductor: Stores energy in a magnetic field; inductance in henries (H), in practice mH and µH. It opposes changes in current. It lets DC through and hinders AC.
  • Inductive reactance: Xl = 2πfL, in ohms. It grows with frequency: inductors block high frequencies, being used in filters and power supplies.
  • RC time constant: In an RC circuit, τ = R×C defines the charge/discharge speed. After ~5τ the capacitor is practically charged. The basis of timing and filters.

Equipment / details

  • Multimeter/capacitance meter: Measures resistance and capacitance to identify and test components.
  • LCR bridge: Precisely measures inductance (L), capacitance (C) and resistance (R).

Step by step

  1. Read the value: Use the color code (resistor) or the marking (capacitor/inductor) to identify the value.
  2. Respect the polarity: On electrolytic/tantalum capacitors, connect + and − correctly; reversal can make the component explode.
  3. Test with the instrument: Measure R with an ohmmeter and C with a capacitance meter, always with the circuit de-energized.

In practice: In a power supply filter, the output electrolytic capacitor smooths the voltage. If installed with reversed polarity, it heats up, swells and can burst — hence the golden rule of checking the polarity band before energizing.

Summary

  • The resistor dissipates; the capacitor stores in an electric field; the inductor in a magnetic field.
  • The capacitor blocks DC and passes AC; the inductor does the opposite.
  • Reactances depend on frequency: Xc falls and Xl rises with f.

a6a7e353-6c2a-46aa-9199-d85ea4a6d093.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The diode is the one-way valve of electronics. From it are born power supplies, indicators and voltage references.

Diodes conduct current in only one direction. This simple property enables rectification, signaling (LED) and regulation (zener).

  • PN junction and biasing: The diode is a PN junction. In forward bias (anode + relative to the cathode) it conducts; in reverse, it blocks. There is a typical forward drop: ~0.7 V in silicon, ~0.3 V in germanium.
  • Rectifier diode: Converts AC into DC by allowing only the favorable half-cycle to pass. Specified by maximum current (If) and maximum reverse voltage (VRRM). E.g.: the 1N400x series.
  • LED (light-emitting diode): Emits light when conducting; the forward drop varies with the color (~1.8 V red to ~3.2 V blue/white). ALWAYS use a current-limiting resistor; typical current 10–20 mA.
  • Zener diode: Designed to operate in reverse bias at a defined breakdown voltage (e.g.: 5.1 V), keeping it stable. Used as a reference and simple regulator, always with a series resistor.
  • Freewheeling diode (flyback): Placed in parallel with coils/relays to drain the current when they are switched off, protecting transistors against the inductive voltage spike.
  • Critical specifications: Pay attention to forward current, reverse voltage, speed (fast/Schottky diodes for switching) and forward drop (Schottky ~0.3 V, lower loss).

Equipment / details

  • Multimeter (diode mode): Checks the forward drop and the polarity of the diode, identifying anode and cathode.
  • Limiting resistor: Protects LEDs and zeners by limiting the current.

Step by step

  1. Identify anode and cathode: The cathode is marked by a band on the body (rectifier) or by the shorter leg/flat (LED).
  2. Test in diode mode: Red probe on the anode and black on the cathode should show ~0.5–0.7 V; reversed, OL (open).
  3. Size the LED resistor: R=(Vsource−Vled)/Iled; e.g.: (5−2)/0.015 ≈ 200 Ω.

In practice: To signal 'energized' on a panel with 12 V and a green LED (≈2.1 V, 15 mA): R=(12−2.1)/0.015≈660 Ω, use a commercial 680 Ω. Without the resistor, the LED receives excessive current and burns out instantly.

Summary

  • A diode conducts in only one direction; forward drop ~0.7 V in silicon.
  • An LED needs a limiting resistor; the drop varies with the color.
  • The zener regulates in reverse bias; the flyback protects against inductive spikes.

30661da2-f105-497d-bea6-21830d20b92d.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The transistor is the component that changed the world: it amplifies and switches signals. It is the heart of every active piece of equipment.

Transistors control a large current/voltage from a small signal. They act as amplifiers or as electronic switches.

  • BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor): It has three terminals: base (B), collector (C) and emitter (E). A small base current controls a much larger collector-emitter current. NPN and PNP types.
  • BJT gain (β/hFE): The collector current is approximately β times the base current: Ic ≈ β×Ib. Typical β values: 100 to 300. A small base current commands a large load current.
  • Regions of operation: Cutoff (off), saturation (fully on, like a closed switch) and active (linear amplification). In switching, cutoff and saturation are used; in audio, the active region.
  • MOSFET: A field-effect transistor controlled by VOLTAGE at the gate terminal; the others are drain (D) and source (S). High input impedance, it consumes almost no gate current. N and P types.
  • BJT vs MOSFET: The BJT is controlled by current; the MOSFET by voltage. MOSFETs dominate power switching (power supplies, dimmers, LED drivers) due to low conduction loss (RDS(on)) and high speed.
  • Transistor as a switch: By driving it into saturation (BJT) or fully conducting (MOSFET), the transistor switches loads (relays, power LEDs, motors) commanded by a microcontroller.
  • Precautions: Respect maximum voltages and currents, use a base resistor (BJT) or gate resistor (MOSFET) and a heatsink when there is power involved. MOSFETs are sensitive to electrostatic discharge (ESD).

Equipment / details

  • Multimeter (diode/hFE mode): Identifies terminals and tests junctions; some measure the hFE of BJTs.
  • Component tester: Automatically identifies the type, pinout and parameters of transistors.

Step by step

  1. Identify the type and pinout: Consult the datasheet to determine NPN/PNP or N/P channel and the terminals.
  2. Define the function: Decide whether it will operate as a switch (cutoff/saturation) or amplifier (active region).
  3. Size the resistors: Calculate the base resistor (BJT) to ensure saturation or the gate resistor (MOSFET) for safe switching.
  4. Test on the bench: Verify conduction by driving the base/gate and measuring the voltage across the load.

In practice: To switch a 12 V LED strip with an Arduino (5 V/few mA output), an N-channel MOSFET is used as a switch: the Arduino pin commands the gate and the MOSFET conducts the strip's current. The microcontroller would never conduct that current directly.

Summary

  • Transistors amplify and switch: a small signal controls a large current.
  • The BJT is commanded by base current; the MOSFET by gate voltage.
  • As a switch, they operate in cutoff/saturation to drive loads via a microcontroller.

30927e3f-4c2c-4115-8fc0-c1597131971d.titulo: ICs, relays, transformers, fuses and regulators
30927e3f-4c2c-4115-8fc0-c1597131971d.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Components that integrate, isolate, transform and protect. They are essential building blocks of any professional equipment.

Here we bring together integration and infrastructure components: integrated circuits, relays, transformers, fuses and voltage regulators.

  • Integrated circuit (IC): Encapsulates thousands to billions of transistors in a single chip. Families: operational amplifiers, logic (74xx, CD4xxx), microcontrollers, regulators. DIP (through-hole) and SMD packages.
  • Operational amplifier (op-amp): Very-high-gain amplifier IC with inverting and non-inverting inputs. Basis of preamplifiers, filters, summers and audio comparators.
  • Relay: Electromechanical switch: a coil, when energized, closes/opens contacts isolated from the control circuit. It lets a low-voltage signal control high-voltage/high-power loads. Always use a freewheeling diode.
  • Transformer: Transfers energy between windings by magnetic induction, stepping AC voltage up or down according to the turns ratio (Vs/Vp = Ns/Np). It provides galvanic isolation between primary and secondary.
  • Fuse: Protection element that breaks when the current exceeds the rated value, interrupting the circuit. Specified by current (A), voltage and type (fast/slow-blow). Never replace it with a value higher than designed.
  • Voltage regulator: Keeps the output stable despite input/load variations. Linear (78xx series for positive, 79xx negative; adjustable LM317) and switching (DC-DC, far more efficient).
  • Dissipation and protection: Linear regulators dissipate the difference (Vin−Vout)×I as heat, requiring a heat sink. Regulators and ICs usually have thermal and short-circuit protection.

Equipment / details

  • Multimeter: Tests fuse continuity, relay contacts and regulator output voltages.
  • Bench power supply: Powers and validates regulators and ICs with controlled voltage.

Step by step

  1. Check the datasheet: Identify the pinout of the IC/regulator and the limits of the relay/transformer/fuse.
  2. Test the fuse: On continuity, a good fuse beeps; a blown one stays open. Replace it with the SAME value.
  3. Verify the regulator: Measure the output voltage with and without load; confirm stability and temperature.
  4. Actuate the relay with protection: Command the coil and confirm the switching; keep the freewheeling diode in place.

In practice: A 7805 regulator delivers a fixed 5 V from 12 V to power the logic of a DMX controller. Since it dissipates (12−5)×I as heat, at higher currents it gets very hot — which is why modern designs prefer a switching DC-DC converter, which is far more efficient.

Summary

  • ICs integrate many transistors; op-amps are the basis of analog audio.
  • Relay and transformer isolate circuits; the transformer also changes the AC voltage.
  • The fuse protects by breaking; regulators stabilize voltage (linear vs. switching).

b49df8b9-022d-4ac8-b880-1d730d10d682.titulo: The multimeter: voltage, current, resistance and continuity
b49df8b9-022d-4ac8-b880-1d730d10d682.conteudo_rico:
To get started: If you could only take one instrument to the job, it would be the multimeter. Let's master every function safely.

The multimeter is the universal measuring tool. Using it correctly — and safely — is a mandatory skill for any technician.

  • Types of multimeter: Analog (needle) and digital (display). The digital one dominates for precision and ease. TRUE RMS versions measure non-sinusoidal signals correctly (essential with switching power supplies and dimmers).
  • Voltage measurement (voltmeter): Select V⎓ (DC) or V∼ (AC). Connect the probes IN PARALLEL with the point to be measured (between two points). The voltmeter has high impedance so as not to disturb the circuit.
  • Current measurement (ammeter): Select A and, crucially, connect the instrument IN SERIES, opening the circuit so the current flows through it. Use the correct terminal (mA or 10A). Classic mistake: measuring current in parallel causes a short circuit.
  • Resistance measurement (ohmmeter): ALWAYS with the circuit DE-ENERGIZED and the component isolated. The instrument injects a small current and reads the resistance. Measured with voltage present? Wrong reading and risk to the device.
  • Continuity: Function with an audible signal (beep) that indicates a closed path (low resistance). Ideal for testing cables, fuses, connections and shorts — always de-energized.
  • Other functions: Many measure capacitance, frequency, temperature, gain (hFE) and have a diode test. Know what your model offers.
  • Safety category (CAT): Multimeters are rated CAT I to IV, indicating where they can be used safely (CAT IV the most severe, close to the mains entrance). Respect the category and the maximum voltage.

Equipment / details

  • Digital multimeter: Measures voltage, current, resistance and continuity.
  • Test probes: Connect the multimeter to the circuit; check the insulation and condition of the probes.

Step by step

  1. Choose the function and range: Select V/A/Ω and DC/AC; on manual models, start with the highest range.
  2. Connect the correct terminals: COM (black) always; red in VΩ for voltage/resistance or in A for current.
  3. Position the probes: Parallel for voltage; series for current; de-energized for Ω and continuity.
  4. Read paying attention to the prefix: Interpret mV/V and mA/A correctly before concluding.

In practice: To find out why a piece of equipment won't turn on, you first measure the voltage at the outlet (parallel), then test the fuse on continuity (de-energized). If the fuse doesn't beep, it's blown — you found the problem without opening anything else.

Summary

  • Voltage is measured in parallel; current in series; resistance de-energized.
  • Continuity (beep) tests cables, fuses and shorts quickly.
  • True RMS and the CAT category are criteria of precision and safety.

aafccaaa-061e-401d-968d-a19a57613a49.conteudo_rico:
To get started: The multimeter gives numbers; the oscilloscope shows the signal alive over time. It is the window into seeing the waveform.

The oscilloscope reveals how voltage varies over time, letting you see noise, distortion, frequency and timing. Test probes and analyzers complete the bench.

  • What the oscilloscope is: An instrument that plots voltage (vertical axis) versus time (horizontal axis). It lets you measure amplitude, period, frequency, rise times and observe the real waveform of the signal.
  • Main controls: Time base (s/div, horizontal axis), vertical scale (V/div), trigger (which stabilizes the image) and coupling (DC shows the average level; AC removes the DC component).
  • Bandwidth and sampling: The bandwidth (MHz) defines the highest frequency faithfully displayed; the sampling rate (on digital ones) must be well above that of the signal. Choose the device according to the signal to be observed.
  • Test probes: The 1x probe passes the signal directly; the 10x attenuates it by 10, increasing the useful impedance and bandwidth and reducing the load on the circuit. Adjust the probe compensation (calibration square) before measuring.
  • Probe grounding: The probe's ground clip connects to the circuit ground. Connecting it to the wrong point can cause a short through the ground — be careful in circuits referenced to the mains.
  • Analyzers: A spectrum analyzer shows the signal in the frequency domain (useful in RF and audio). A logic analyzer captures multiple simultaneous digital signals to debug buses.
  • Typical measurements at events: Checking noise on audio lines, integrity of DMX/digital signals, presence of oscillation, waveforms of switching power supplies.

Equipment / details

  • Oscilloscope: Visualizes and measures waveforms over time.
  • 10x test probe: Measures with less load on the circuit and greater bandwidth; requires compensation.
  • Spectrum/logic analyzer: Analyzes signals in the frequency domain or digital buses.

Step by step

  1. Adjust the probe: Select 10x and compensate on the calibration output until the square wave has straight corners.
  2. Connect ground and probe: Ground clip to the circuit ground; probe to the measurement point.
  3. Configure scales and trigger: Adjust V/div and s/div to frame the wave and use the trigger to stabilize the image.
  4. Measure the parameters: Read amplitude (Vpp), period and calculate the frequency (f=1/T).

In practice: When investigating a hum on an audio line, the oscilloscope shows a 60 Hz sine wave added to the signal — a classic symptom of a ground loop. The multimeter's number wouldn't reveal the shape; the oscilloscope diagnoses it immediately.

Summary

  • The oscilloscope shows voltage vs. time: amplitude, period and waveform.
  • The 10x probe loads the circuit less; always compensate before measuring.
  • Spectrum analyzers (frequency) and logic analyzers (digital buses) complement it.

914feec5-39ba-4441-8420-3dcb654d220a.titulo: Analog vs. digital, logic levels and binary
914feec5-39ba-4441-8420-3dcb654d220a.conteudo_rico:
To get started: The world is analog, but modern equipment thinks in binary. Understanding this boundary is understanding today's electronics.

Analog signals vary continuously; digital ones take discrete values (0 and 1). This lesson establishes the basis of digital reasoning.

  • Analog signal: Varies continuously over time and can take infinite values within a range. E.g.: the voltage of a microphone, the position of a fader. Sensitive to noise, which adds to the signal.
  • Digital signal: Takes discrete values, usually two: 0 and 1 (binary). More robust to noise, since the receiver only needs to distinguish 'high' from 'low'. The basis of computers and modern equipment.
  • Logic levels: Each family defines voltage ranges for 0 and 1. In 5 V logic (TTL), ~0 V is the low level and ~5 V is high, with a forbidden band in the middle. There is 3.3 V logic and others; mixing voltages requires attention.
  • Binary system: Base 2, with digits 0 and 1 (bits). Each position is worth a power of 2. E.g.: 1011₂ = 8+0+2+1 = 11. Computers operate natively in binary.
  • Bits, bytes and multiples: 8 bits = 1 byte. A byte represents 0 to 255 (256 values). Multiples: kilobyte, megabyte, etc. The number of bits defines the resolution and the range of values.
  • Hexadecimal: Base 16 (0–9, A–F) used to represent binary compactly: each hex digit equals 4 bits. Common in addresses and colors (e.g.: #FF0000).
  • Positive and negative logic: In positive logic, high level = 1; in negative logic, high = 0. Knowing the convention avoids interpreting signals backwards.

Equipment / details

  • Multimeter/oscilloscope: Checks logic levels (high/low) and the shape of the digital signal.
  • Logic analyzer: Captures bit sequences on digital buses.

Step by step

  1. Identify the type of signal: Determine whether the line is analog (continuous variation) or digital (high/low).
  2. Know the logic voltage: Check whether it is 5 V or 3.3 V so as not to damage inputs.
  3. Convert between bases: Practice binary↔decimal↔hexadecimal conversions.
  4. Measure the levels: Confirm with the instrument that '1' and '0' are within valid ranges.

In practice: When connecting a 5 V sensor to a 3.3 V microcontroller, applying the 5 V high level directly can damage the input. The solution is a voltage divider or a logic-level converter — a frequent practical issue in integrations.

Summary

  • Analog varies continuously; digital uses discrete values (0/1), more immune to noise.
  • Binary is base 2; hexadecimal compacts 4 bits per digit.
  • Logic levels define ranges for 0 and 1; mixing 5 V and 3.3 V requires attention.

6ea276c0-7ef9-4157-bb39-e15192320d65.conteudo_rico:
To get started: From logic gates all digital processing is born; from sampling, digital audio and video are born. Let's unite the two worlds.

Logic gates implement decisions in hardware; ADC and DAC bridge the analog and the digital world, essential in audio and video.

  • Basic logic gates: They implement Boolean functions. NOT (inverts), AND (1 only if all inputs are 1), OR (1 if any is 1). They are the building blocks of every digital circuit.
  • Derived gates: NAND (negated AND), NOR (negated OR), XOR (1 when the inputs differ) and XNOR. NAND and NOR are 'universal': any function can be built with them alone.
  • Truth table: Lists all input combinations and the corresponding output. It is the rigorous way to define and verify the behavior of a gate or logic circuit.
  • Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC): Transforms an analog signal into digital numbers through two processes: sampling (measuring at instants) and quantization (rounding to discrete levels).
  • Sampling theorem (Nyquist): To reconstruct a signal without loss, the sampling rate must be greater than twice the highest frequency of the signal. That's why audio (up to ~20 kHz) uses 44.1 kHz or 48 kHz.
  • Quantization and bit resolution: The number of bits defines how many amplitude levels exist: 16 bits = 65,536 levels. More bits = less quantization error and greater dynamic range. Professional audio uses 24 bits.
  • Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC): Does the reverse path: converts the numbers back into analog voltage, reconstructing the sound or video at the equipment's output.

Equipment / details

  • Logic ICs / microcontroller: Implement logic gates and contain internal ADC/DAC.
  • Oscilloscope: Compares the original analog signal with the one reconstructed by the DAC.

Step by step

  1. Build the logic function: Define the truth table and choose the gates (or universal NAND/NOR).
  2. Set the sampling rate: Ensure a rate greater than twice the highest frequency of the signal (Nyquist).
  3. Choose the resolution: Select the number of bits according to the desired dynamic range.
  4. Check the reconstruction: Compare the DAC output with the input signal on the oscilloscope.

In practice: A digital sound console samples each input at 48 kHz with 24 bits. Respecting Nyquist (>2×20 kHz) and using enough bits is what guarantees faithful audio, without aliasing or audible quantization noise.

Summary

  • Logic gates implement decisions; NAND and NOR are universal.
  • The ADC samples (Nyquist: >2× the highest frequency) and quantizes (bits = levels).
  • The DAC reconstructs the analog signal from the digital numbers.

bc4b2fac-0da8-4c3b-b3ba-505994468a1a.conteudo_rico:
To get started: At the event, the signal is only as good as the cable that carries it. Knowing cables, connectors and impedance separates the amateur from the professional.

Each type of signal calls for a suitable cable and connector. Impedance and cable construction determine quality and reach.

  • Anatomy of the cable: Conductor (copper), insulation, shielding (braid/foil) and jacket. The shield protects against external interference and must be well grounded at the correct ends.
  • Characteristic impedance: A property of transmission cables (coaxial, network) that defines how the signal propagates, in ohms. E.g.: video coaxial 75 Ω, antenna 50 Ω. Matching the impedance avoids reflections.
  • Reflections and matching: When the impedance of the source, cable and load do not match, part of the signal is reflected, causing loss and distortion (important in SDI video, RF and DMX, which requires 120 Ω termination).
  • Audio connectors: XLR (3 pins, balanced, microphones and professional line), 1/4' phone/TRS (balanced or stereo), 1/4' phone/TS (unbalanced), RCA (unbalanced, consumer), 3.5 mm mini-jack.
  • Video/data connectors: BNC (video/SDI, 75 Ω, bayonet locking), HDMI, RJ45 (Ethernet network), USB, DisplayPort, optical fiber for long distances.
  • Gauge and length: Long cables have more resistance and capacitance, attenuating the signal. For power (speakers), a larger gauge (smaller AWG) reduces losses; for signal, shielding and quality matter more.
  • Cabling care: Avoid poorly made splices, respect the minimum bend radius, don't run signal cable alongside power cable, and label/test the cables before the show.

Equipment / details

  • Cable tester: Checks continuity and pin mapping of XLR, phone, RJ45 cables, etc.
  • Multimeter: Measures continuity of the shield and the conductors.

Step by step

  1. Choose the cable by the signal: Select 75 Ω coaxial for video, twisted pair for network, shielded for audio.
  2. Check the impedance: Ensure matching between source, cable and load; terminate what requires termination (DMX 120 Ω).
  3. Connect and lock: Use the correct connector and lock it (XLR/BNC) to avoid drops during the event.
  4. Test before the show: Run all cables through the tester to catch bad contact or pin reversal.

In practice: On a long DMX line without the 120 Ω termination resistor at the last device, the signal reflects and causes random flickering in the lights. Adding the correct termination solves it — a direct example of impedance matching in practice.

Summary

  • Each signal calls for its own cable and connector; XLR and BNC lock and are professional.
  • Characteristic impedance must match to avoid reflections and losses.
  • Shielding, gauge and prior testing ensure reliability on stage.

64999c14-61eb-4da4-a555-a9a004c3596c.titulo: Balanced vs. unbalanced and signal grounding
64999c14-61eb-4da4-a555-a9a004c3596c.conteudo_rico:
To get started: Why does the professional microphone cable have three pins and pick up almost no noise? The answer lies in the balanced signal.

Balanced lines reject noise by design; understanding the concept explains professional audio and data connections, and the role of the signal ground.

  • Unbalanced signal: Uses two conductors: signal and ground/shield. Simple and cheap, but the shield is also the reference, picking up noise on long cables. E.g.: RCA, TS phone, instruments.
  • Balanced signal: Uses three conductors: two for the signal (in opposite phase) and one ground/shield. The signal travels as the difference between the two conductors.
  • Common-mode rejection (CMRR): The picked-up noise adds equally to both conductors (common mode). At the receiver, when subtracting one from the other, the signal doubles and the noise cancels out. That's why balanced lines run long distances with low noise.
  • Where it's used: Balanced: microphones, console outputs, long runs (XLR, TRS). Unbalanced: short distances, home equipment, instruments (with a short cable).
  • Signal ground vs. protective ground: The signal ground is the 0 V reference of the circuit; the protective ground (PE) is electrical safety. Confusing them or interconnecting them at several points creates ground loops and hum.
  • Ground lift: On equipment with a loop problem, some have a 'ground lift' switch that disconnects the signal ground from the chassis, breaking the loop — without ever removing the protective ground from the mains.
  • Direct boxes (DI): Convert an unbalanced high-impedance signal (instrument) into a balanced low-impedance one, allowing long runs with low noise and isolating grounds.

Equipment / details

  • Direct box (DI): Balances and isolates instrument signals for the console.
  • Multimeter/oscilloscope: Identifies hum and ground potential differences between equipment.

Step by step

  1. Classify the line: See whether the connection is balanced (XLR/TRS) or unbalanced (RCA/TS).
  2. Prefer balanced on long runs: Use balanced cables for microphones and long runs, reducing noise.
  3. Treat the ground carefully: Keep the protective ground; use a DI or ground lift to solve signal loops.
  4. Check for hum: If there is 60 Hz hum, suspect a ground loop and isolate.

In practice: A keyboard 20 m from the console, connected by an unbalanced cable, arrives full of hum. Switching to a DI box (balancing the signal and isolating the ground), the noise disappears — the standard live-audio solution.

Summary

  • Balanced uses two conductors in opposition and cancels noise by subtraction (CMRR).
  • Unbalanced is simple but picks up noise over long distances.
  • Signal ground and protective ground are distinct; ground loops cause hum — treat with DI/lift, never removing the PE.

b6b6b751-a8b2-4c04-b949-994e7047bc37.titulo: Protocols: DMX512, MIDI, USB, Ethernet/IP, Art-Net/sACN and wireless
b6b6b751-a8b2-4c04-b949-994e7047bc37.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Modern events are data networks. Mastering the lighting, audio and control protocols is what ties the whole show together.

Each protocol carries a type of information with its own rules for wiring, speed and addressing. Knowing them lets you integrate light, sound and video.

  • DMX512: The standard stage-lighting protocol. Serial, based on RS-485, over twisted pair with a 5-pin XLR connector (sometimes 3). A universe has 512 channels; each fixture takes a start address and uses N channels. Requires 120 Ω termination.
  • MIDI: The musical instrument protocol (Musical Instrument Digital Interface). Serial at 31,250 bps, 5-pin DIN connector (or via USB). It transmits messages such as note, velocity, control and program change — it does not transmit audio, only commands.
  • USB: Universal Serial Bus for connecting peripherals (audio interfaces, controllers, flash drives). It provides data and power (5 V). Several generations with increasing speeds; it recognizes devices automatically (plug and play).
  • Ethernet/IP: A standard data network (RJ45/twisted pair or fiber) over which event protocols travel. It allows many lighting universes and audio channels over a single cable, with managed switches.
  • Art-Net and sACN: Protocols that carry DMX over an Ethernet/IP network. Art-Net and sACN (E1.31) allow hundreds of universes over one network cable, simplifying large lighting and media rigs.
  • Audio over IP: Standards such as Dante, AES67 and AVB carry many channels of digital audio over an Ethernet network with low latency, replacing large analog multicore cables.
  • Wireless (RF and IR): RF (radio) for wireless microphones, in-ears and remote control; it operates in licensed/unlicensed bands and suffers from interference and spectrum congestion. IR (infrared) requires line of sight, used in remotes and some systems.

Equipment / details

  • DMX/USB interface and Art-Net node: Connects the computer to the lighting network.
  • Managed network switch: Reliably carries Art-Net/sACN and audio over IP.
  • Network analyzer/monitor: Checks the traffic and addressing of the protocols.

Step by step

  1. Choose the protocol by function: Light: DMX/Art-Net/sACN; audio: Dante/AES67; instruments: MIDI.
  2. Address the fixtures: Assign a DMX start address and universe; set the IPs on the network.
  3. Cable it correctly: Twisted pair/RJ45 and termination where required (DMX 120 Ω).
  4. Verify communication: Confirm the fixtures respond and there are no address/IP conflicts.

In practice: On a large tour, instead of dozens of DMX cables, a single network cable carries sACN with several universes to nodes that convert it into DMX at the light points. Less cable, less weight, faster setup.

Summary

  • DMX512 controls light (512 channels/universe, RS-485, 120 Ω termination).
  • MIDI sends musical commands; USB and Ethernet/IP connect and carry data.
  • Art-Net/sACN carry DMX over the network; Dante/AES67 carry audio; RF/IR cover wireless.

e8abc4e7-4ad3-4799-a9a8-bed9b3892b7d.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Clean sound starts with matching levels and impedances. Here you learn to speak the language of analog audio.

The concepts of impedance, gain and line levels determine whether the signal arrives clean, distorted or noisy at the output.

  • Impedance in audio: Opposition to AC current, in ohms. Rule of thumb: a low-impedance source feeds a high-impedance input (voltage-bridging match). Microphones and professional outputs are low impedance.
  • Loudspeaker impedance: Speakers have a nominal impedance (4, 8, 16 Ω). Wiring speakers in parallel lowers the impedance and demands more from the amplifier; respecting the amp's minimum load avoids overheating and protection tripping.
  • Gain: How much the signal is amplified, expressed in dB. Positive gain amplifies; negative attenuates. The input gain stage (gain/trim) adjusts the signal level to the console's internal standard.
  • Decibel (dB): A logarithmic unit for the ratio between powers/voltages. +6 dB ≈ double the voltage; +10 dB ≈ a perceived 'double the volume'. dBu and dBV are voltage references; dBFS is a digital reference (0 dBFS = maximum).
  • Signal levels: Mic level (very low, µV–mV, needs preamplification), line level (operating level, ~+4 dBu professional or −10 dBV consumer), instrument level and speaker level (high, post-amplification).
  • Gain staging: Setting each stage to operate at a good level, away from noise (floor) and away from clipping (ceiling). Correct gain staging maximizes the signal-to-noise ratio and dynamic range.
  • Clipping and headroom: Clipping is the flattening of the signal when it exceeds the maximum, producing distortion. Headroom is the margin between the working level and the maximum; keeping headroom avoids distortion on peaks.

Equipment / details

  • Mixing console / preamplifier: Applies gain and adjusts levels from mic to line.
  • Level meter (VU/PPM): Monitors level and headroom to avoid clipping.

Step by step

  1. Identify the source level: See whether it is mic, instrument or line to choose the right input.
  2. Adjust the input gain: Raise the trim to a good level, with peaks below clipping (headroom).
  3. Impedance matching: Ensure a low-impedance source into a high-impedance input.
  4. Check the amplifier load: Add up the speaker impedances and respect the amp's minimum.

In practice: If the microphone trim is too low, you raise the following fader and gain, amplifying the background noise along with it — a weak signal and hiss. Setting the input gain correctly (gain staging) fixes the signal-to-noise ratio at the source.

Summary

  • In audio, a low-impedance source feeds a high-impedance input.
  • dB is logarithmic; levels range from mic (weak) to speaker (strong), passing through line.
  • Good gain staging maximizes signal-to-noise while keeping headroom against clipping.

6f6aee51-d35b-42c7-aef0-e266949b104c.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The +48V button and the fight against noise define the quality of the captured sound. Let's understand both in depth.

Phantom power energizes condenser microphones; and noise control determines audio clarity. Two pillars of professional capture.

  • Phantom power +48V: A 48 V DC voltage sent by the console over the same balanced XLR cable to power condenser microphones and active DIs. It is 'phantom' because it travels equally on both signal conductors, without affecting the audio.
  • Who needs it and who doesn't: Condenser microphones and active DIs NEED phantom. Dynamic microphones do NOT — and, although they usually tolerate it, connecting/disconnecting with phantom on can cause pops and, on sensitive gear (a poorly cabled ribbon), cause damage.
  • Good phantom practices: Turn phantom on only after connecting the microphone and with the fader/channel muted; turn it off before disconnecting. It avoids pops and protects the loudspeakers.
  • Electronic noise: Every circuit generates noise: thermal (agitation of electrons, unavoidable), the components' background, and noise picked up from the environment (EMI, 60 Hz mains). The preamplifier is where noise weighs most, since it amplifies everything downstream.
  • Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR): The ratio between the signal level and the background noise, in dB. The higher it is, the cleaner the audio. Good gain staging and balanced cables raise the SNR.
  • Types of audible interference: Hum at 60 Hz from a ground loop; hiss from excessive gain/thermal noise; RF interference (cell phones, transmitters). Each has its own cause and treatment.
  • Noise floor and dynamic range: The noise floor is the minimum audible level of the system; dynamic range is the distance between the floor and the maximum level without distortion. Professional systems aim for a low floor and wide dynamic range.

Equipment / details

  • Condenser microphone: Requires phantom +48V to operate.
  • Console with phantom and meters: Supplies +48V and monitors level and noise.

Step by step

  1. Connect with the channel muted: Plug in the microphone with the fader down and the channel muted.
  2. Enable phantom if needed: Turn +48V on only for condensers/active DI; leave it off for dynamics.
  3. Adjust the gain aiming for SNR: Raise the trim to a good level, keeping the background noise well below the signal.
  4. Turn it off before disconnecting: Disable phantom and lower the channel before removing the microphone.

In practice: A 'silent' condenser microphone is usually a lack of phantom +48V. Turning on the button on the console brings it to life. A constant 60 Hz hum, on the other hand, points to a ground loop, not a lack of phantom — different diagnoses for different symptoms.

Summary

  • Phantom +48V powers condenser microphones over the balanced XLR.
  • Handle phantom with the channel muted; dynamics do not need it.
  • SNR measures how clean the audio is; hum, hiss and RF have distinct causes and solutions.

6803635c-62e7-47e5-af53-f07f039630ec.titulo: Video signals: HDMI, SDI and A/D conversion
6803635c-62e7-47e5-af53-f07f039630ec.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Big screens, LED walls and projection depend on reliable video signals. HDMI and SDI are the two worlds you need to master.

Professional video travels mainly over HDMI and SDI, each with its advantages. Understanding their characteristics avoids black screens at the critical moment.

  • Analog vs. digital video: Video migrated from analog (composite, VGA, component) to digital (HDMI, SDI, DisplayPort). Digital carries the image and, in some cases, audio, with stable quality up to the limit of its reach.
  • HDMI: A consumer/professional standard that carries digital video and audio over a single cable. Pros: ubiquitous, carries audio. Cons: short reach (a few meters without extenders), a connector with no lock (falls out easily) and the use of HDCP (copy protection).
  • SDI (Serial Digital Interface): A professional broadcast standard over 75 Ω coaxial cable with a locking BNC connector. Pros: long distances (tens to hundreds of meters), a secure connector. Standards: SD-SDI, HD-SDI, 3G/6G/12G-SDI depending on resolution/rate.
  • Resolution and rate: Resolution (e.g., 1920×1080 'Full HD', 3840×2160 '4K') and frame rate (fps). Higher resolution/fps demands more bandwidth on the cable — hence 3G/6G/12G-SDI and higher HDMI versions.
  • A/D conversion in video: Cameras and capture digitize light via a sensor; the signal can be converted between formats by converters (HDMI↔SDI), scalers (change resolution) and processors, essential to match sources and screens.
  • Signal extension: To cover distance, HDMI extenders over twisted pair/fiber are used, or simply SDI (which already goes far). Active cables and fiber solve long video runs.
  • Sync and processing: In systems with several sources/screens, processors and genlock keep the sync, avoiding tearing and dropouts when switching sources.

Equipment / details

  • HDMI↔SDI converter: Adapts the signal between the consumer world (HDMI) and broadcast (SDI).
  • Scaler/video processor: Adjusts resolution and combines/switches sources with sync.

Step by step

  1. Choose the standard by distance: Short: HDMI; long/secure: SDI or HDMI over fiber.
  2. Match resolution and rate: Ensure the source, cable and screen support the desired resolution/fps.
  3. Convert when needed: Use an HDMI↔SDI converter to integrate different equipment.
  4. Test the chain beforehand: Check the image end to end before the event, including source switching.

In practice: To carry the image from a laptop (HDMI) to a big screen 60 m away, you convert HDMI to SDI, run the coax to the stage and convert back to the screen's input. SDI covers the distance that plain HDMI could not reach.

Summary

  • HDMI is ubiquitous and carries audio, but has short reach and a non-locking connector.
  • SDI covers long distances with a locking BNC over 75 Ω coax.
  • Converters and scalers integrate sources; always test the chain end to end.

e46ec519-3289-449a-933f-ab4004fac5cf.conteudo_rico:
To begin: An unexplained black screen at the event? Very often the culprit is EDID or HDCP. Understanding these two 'handshakes' saves the show.

EDID and HDCP are automatic negotiations between source and screen. When they fail, the image disappears — so the technician needs to know them.

  • What EDID is: Extended Display Identification Data: data the monitor/screen sends to the source informing its capabilities (resolutions, rates, supported audio). The source uses the EDID to choose the format to send.
  • EDID problems: If the EDID is not read (bad cable, distributor, switch), the source may send an incompatible format or none — resulting in a black screen or the wrong resolution. Distributors/matrices sometimes need EDID management/emulation.
  • What HDCP is: High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection: protection against copying content (movies, streaming). Source and screen perform a cryptographic 'handshake'; they only display if both are compatible and authorized.
  • HDCP problems: If some equipment in the chain (switch, converter, capture) does not support HDCP, protected content will not pass — it produces a black screen or an error warning. Capturing/distributing protected content may be blocked by design.
  • Overview and legality: HDCP is copyright protection; bypassing it for piracy is illegal. The technician must use compatible equipment and their own/licensed content, not 'break' the protection.
  • Practical diagnosis: An intermittent black screen when switching sources suggests EDID; a black screen only with protected content (streaming, Blu-ray) suggests HDCP. Identifying the symptom directs the solution.
  • Good practices: Use quality cables and distributors with EDID management and HDCP support; test the chain with the real content before the event; keep compatible converters as backup.

Equipment / details

  • Distributor/matrix with EDID management: Keeps the handshake stable between sources and screens.
  • HDCP-compatible converter: Ensures display of protected content in the chain.

Step by step

  1. Check the EDID reading: Confirm the source recognizes the screen (correct resolution detected).
  2. Check HDCP support: Ensure all equipment in the chain supports HDCP for protected content.
  3. Diagnose by symptom: Failure when switching sources → EDID; failure only with protected content → HDCP.
  4. Test with the real content: Play the event material on the complete chain before the show.

In practice: A streaming video shows a black screen on the big screen while a slide from the laptop appears normally. The slide is not protected; the stream is. The culprit is a converter without HDCP in the chain — replacing it with a compatible one solves it.

Summary

  • EDID tells the source what the screen supports; failures produce a black screen or the wrong format.
  • HDCP is copy protection via handshake; incompatible equipment blocks the content.
  • Diagnose by symptom and use compatible equipment — without circumventing the protection.

b3c14119-7635-4416-84a6-7eb644dc45c3.titulo: Rectification, filtering and linear vs. switched power supplies
b3c14119-7635-4416-84a6-7eb644dc45c3.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Almost every piece of equipment converts the 127/220 V from the outlet into low-voltage DC. Understanding the power supply is understanding the heart of the power system.

The power supply transforms the mains AC into stable DC. Knowing its stages and types allows you to diagnose and size the system's power.

  • Stages of a linear power supply: Transformer (steps down the AC voltage and isolates) → rectifier (AC→pulsating DC) → filter (smooths) → regulator (stabilizes). Each stage has its own function and point of failure.
  • Rectification: Diodes convert AC into pulsating DC. Half-wave uses 1 diode (uses half of the cycle); full-wave uses a bridge of 4 diodes, using both half-cycles — more efficient and with less ripple.
  • Filtering: The filter capacitor after the rectifier stores charge and 'smooths' the pulsating voltage, reducing the ripple (residual fluctuation). The greater the capacitance and the lower the current, the smaller the ripple.
  • Regulation: The regulator keeps the output constant despite variations in input and load (e.g., 78xx, LM317). It ensures a stable voltage for sensitive electronics.
  • Linear vs. switched power supply: Linear: simple, low noise, but heavy and inefficient (dissipates heat). Switched (SMPS): converts at high frequency, is light, efficient and versatile, but can generate switching noise (EMI) if poorly designed.
  • Why switched supplies dominate: Modern equipment, UPS units and notebook/LED power supplies use SMPS for their efficiency and reduced size. In sensitive audio, a low-noise linear supply is still valued in certain stages.
  • Power supply diagnosis: Symptoms: high ripple (hum/instability) suggests a dried-out filter capacitor; zero output may be a fuse/rectifier; wrong voltage points to the regulator. The oscilloscope reveals ripple that the multimeter hides.

Equipment / details

  • Power supply / SMPS: Converts mains AC into DC for the equipment.
  • Oscilloscope: Measures the ripple and checks the quality of the DC.

Step by step

  1. Identify the stages: Locate the transformer, rectifier bridge, filter capacitor and regulator.
  2. Measure the DC output: Confirm the correct voltage with the multimeter set to V⏸.
  3. Check the ripple: Use the oscilloscope in AC coupling to measure the residual fluctuation.
  4. Isolate the fault: High ripple → filter; zero output → fuse/rectifier; wrong voltage → regulator.

In practice: A piece of equipment starts to 'hum' and freeze: when measuring the supply, the oscilloscope shows high ripple. The electrolytic filter capacitor dried out over time. Replacing it restores clean DC — a classic defect in old equipment.

Summary

  • Linear supply: transforms, rectifies, filters and regulates AC into stable DC.
  • A full-wave bridge and filter capacitor reduce the ripple.
  • SMPS is light and efficient (dominates today); linear is low-noise but heavy.

c1207bf4-c746-41da-80d2-a8c0b3d30d2f.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Reliable and safe power is the invisible foundation of the event. Grounding, UPS and surge protection prevent accidents and outages.

This lesson covers the electrical infrastructure that keeps the show standing: correct grounding, uninterruptible power and surge protection.

  • Role of grounding: The protective conductor (PE, ground wire) carries the fault current to earth, making the protection trip and keeping the chassis at a safe potential. It is a SAFETY item, not optional.
  • Schemes and equipotential bonding: At events, equipment must share the same ground reference (equipotential bonding) to avoid potential differences between racks, which cause shocks and ground loops.
  • UPS: Uninterruptible Power Supply: provides battery power when the mains fails, avoiding an abrupt shutdown of digital consoles, computers and processors. Types: standby, line-interactive and online (double conversion, best for critical loads).
  • Autonomy and sizing: The UPS is sized by the power (VA/W) of the loads and the desired runtime. Overloading it reduces runtime or shuts it down; undersizing leaves critical loads unprotected.
  • SPD — surge protection: Surge Protection Device: diverts voltage spikes (lightning, grid switching) to earth, protecting equipment. Installed in the panel and/or in power strips. It needs good grounding to work.
  • Cascaded protection: Combines circuit breakers (overcurrent), RCD/GFCI (leakage current — protection against shock) and SPD (surge). Each protects against a different risk; together they form the electrical defense.
  • Good practices at events: Distribute loads by balancing the phases, do not exceed the capacity of the circuits, use suitable cables and connections, ensure ground at all outlets and protect critical loads with UPS + SPD.

Equipment / details

  • UPS: Keeps critical loads powered during mains outages.
  • SPD and RCD: Protect against surges and against leakage currents (shock).
  • Earth tester / multimeter: Checks the quality of the grounding and the presence of ground at the outlets.

Step by step

  1. Check the ground: Confirm the presence and continuity of the ground at all outlets used.
  2. Bond the racks: Ensure a common ground reference between equipment to avoid loops.
  3. Size the UPS: Add up the power of the critical loads and choose a UPS with margin and adequate runtime.
  4. Install cascaded protection: Combine circuit breaker, RCD and SPD, all with effective grounding.

In practice: On a digital console powered directly from the mains, a power outage brings everything down in the middle of the show. With an online UPS, the console stays on the battery and the operator has time to manage the situation — essential protection for critical loads.

Summary

  • Grounding is safety: it carries the fault to earth and keeps chassis safe.
  • A UPS keeps critical loads running during a power outage; size it by power and runtime.
  • The SPD diverts surges (depends on good ground); circuit breaker, RCD and SPD protect distinct risks.

468b1025-fbb1-4e39-aae9-ab3c3245569a.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Mysterious hums, hisses and lockups almost always share the same root: interference. Learn to prevent it and to fight it.

EMI is electromagnetic interference; EMC is the ability to coexist without causing it or suffering from it. Shielding, layout and grounding treatment are the technician's weapons.

  • EMI and EMC: EMI (Electromagnetic Interference) is unwanted energy that disturbs circuits. EMC (Electromagnetic Compatibility) is the design that lets equipment work without causing or suffering excessive interference.
  • Coupling modes: Interference couples through conduction (via wires/ground), through the electric field (capacitive), through the magnetic field (inductive) and through radiation (waves). Identifying the mode points to the solution.
  • Typical sources at events: Switching power supplies, dimmers (they chop the sine wave and generate noise), motors, RF transmitters, cell phones, lamps and poorly arranged cabling itself.
  • Shielding: Wrapping the signal in a grounded braid/foil blocks electric fields and radiation. Shielding only works well if properly grounded (generally at one end for audio, to avoid a loop).
  • Ground loop: It occurs when there is more than one ground path between pieces of equipment, forming an 'antenna' in which a 60 Hz current circulates, generating hum. It is the number 1 cause of hum in audio/video systems.
  • Fighting the ground loop: Bond everything to the same potential; use a single ground reference; isolate with a DI box, isolation transformer or optical converters; use a ground lift on the signal — NEVER remove the protective earth from the mains.
  • Layout and separation: Physically separate power and signal cables; cross them at 90° when unavoidable; keep signal cables short and away from EMI sources; use balanced, quality cables.

Equipment / details

  • Isolation transformer / DI: Breaks ground loops by galvanically isolating the signal.
  • Oscilloscope / analyzer: Identifies the frequency and nature of the interference.

Step by step

  1. Characterize the interference: 60 Hz hum → ground loop; RF hiss → nearby transmitter; dimmer noise → switching supply/dimmer.
  2. Check the cabling: Move signal away from power, check shields and ground connections.
  3. Isolate the loop: Use a DI/isolation transformer or a ground lift on the signal, keeping the PE.
  4. Confirm the improvement: Measure/listen again to validate the solution.

In practice: A system with clean sound starts to hum when the projector is plugged into an outlet on another circuit. A ground loop has appeared between the pieces of equipment. Powering everything from the same point (bonding it to the same potential) or isolating the video signal makes the hum disappear.

Summary

  • EMI is the interference; EMC is designing to coexist with it.
  • Grounded shielding and good layout prevent noise coupling.
  • A ground loop causes 60 Hz hum; solve it by isolating the signal, never by cutting the protective earth.

79052618-0256-4a24-9536-441e792ab5c8.conteudo_rico:
To begin: A good solder joint is shiny, solid and reliable; a bad one brings the show down. Let's learn to solder like a pro.

Soldering is joining components electrically with a molten metal alloy. Correct technique ensures durable connections; poor technique creates intermittent faults.

  • What solder is: A metal alloy (traditionally tin-lead Sn-Pb; today also lead-free) that, melted by the iron, creates an electrical and mechanical junction between the component and the board/terminal.
  • Soldering iron and temperature: The iron/station heats the tip to a controlled temperature (typically 300–370 °C). Keep the tip clean and tinned; insufficient temperature produces a cold joint, excessive temperature damages components and board.
  • Flux: A substance that cleans and removes oxides, letting the solder 'wet' and flow well. Electronic solder already comes with flux in its core; extra flux helps with rework.
  • Correct technique: Heat the terminal AND the pad of the board SIMULTANEOUSLY with the tip; apply the solder to the heated point (not to the tip of the iron); let it flow; remove the solder and then the iron. A good joint is smooth, shiny and concave.
  • Common defects: Cold joint (dull, grainy, poor contact), excess solder (bridges/shorts), insufficient solder, overheating (lifted pad), bridges between traces. They cause intermittent faults that are hard to find.
  • Through-hole vs. SMD: Components with leads (through-hole) are easier to solder by hand; SMD (surface mount) are tiny and require specific technique/tools (hot air, solder paste).
  • Desoldering and rework: To remove components, use a solder sucker, desoldering braid or hot-air station. Heat, remove the solder and extract without damaging the board.
  • Safety and health: Solder in a ventilated place (flux fumes are irritating), use a fume extractor, avoid touching the hot tip, wash your hands after handling leaded solder and dispose of waste properly.

Equipment / details

  • Soldering station: Heats and controls the tip temperature for soldering.
  • Sucker / desoldering braid: Removes solder during rework and desoldering.
  • Fume extractor: Captures flux fumes, protecting the airways.

Step by step

  1. Prepare and tin the tip: Heat it, clean it on the sponge/wool and apply a little solder to the tip.
  2. Heat the joint: Touch the tip to the terminal and the pad at the same time for 1–2 s.
  3. Apply the solder to the point: Touch the solder to the heated joint (not to the tip) and let enough flow.
  4. Remove and inspect: Remove solder and iron; the joint should be smooth and shiny. Check for bridges and poor contact.

In practice: A piece of equipment that fails 'when you wiggle the cable' usually has a dull cold joint on a connector. Reheating the joint with the correct technique, forming a shiny, concave solder joint, eliminates the intermittent fault.

Summary

  • A good joint bonds mechanically and electrically; it is smooth, shiny and concave.
  • Heat the joint (not the solder on the tip) and use flux for good wetting.
  • Know the defects (cold joint, bridges) and solder with ventilation and safety.

81c37fa5-396b-41d9-96a0-299ceb8d564d.titulo: Electrical safety and introduction to NR-10
81c37fa5-396b-41d9-96a0-299ceb8d564d.conteudo_rico:
To begin: No show is worth an accident. This lesson closes the course with the most important topic: working with electricity without getting hurt.

Electrical safety is an absolute priority. Here you learn the risks, the protective measures and the basis of the Brazilian standard NR-10.

  • Risks of electricity: Electric shock (current through the body affects muscles and the heart — from just a few tens of mA there is already serious risk), burns, arc flash and fire. The severity depends on the current, the path and the time.
  • What determines the shock: By Ohm's law, the current in the body depends on the voltage and the resistance (dry vs. wet skin). That is why damp environments and sweaty hands greatly increase the danger.
  • Collective and individual protective measures: Collective: de-energization, grounding, signaling, insulation. Individual (PPE): insulating gloves, suitable footwear, goggles. Collective protection takes priority over individual protection.
  • Safe de-energization: Basic sequence: switch off the source, prevent re-energization (lockout/tagout), verify the absence of voltage, ground where applicable and signal. Only then is it safe to work.
  • NR-10 — overview: Regulatory Standard No. 10 of Brazil: it establishes safety requirements for electrical installations and services. It applies to those who design, assemble, operate and maintain electrical installations, protecting workers' health.
  • NR-10 requirements: It includes risk-control measures, up-to-date diagrams, procedures, adequate PPE/CPE and MANDATORY training: NR-10 basic course (minimum 40 h) for those working in the risk zone; work on high voltage requires a supplementary course.
  • Application at events: Stage setups deal with a lot of energy in a short time and an improvised environment: earth on every outlet, properly sized cables, protection (circuit breaker/RCD/SPD), trained crew and procedures are mandatory, not optional.
  • First aid: In a shock, do NOT touch a victim still connected to the power: switch off the source first. Call for help; knowing CPR can save lives. Prevention, however, is always the best strategy.

Equipment / details

  • PPE (insulating gloves, footwear, goggles): Protects the worker during electrical interventions.
  • Voltage detector / multimeter: Verifies the absence of voltage before touching the circuit.
  • Lockout and tagout system: Prevents accidental re-energization during the work.

Step by step

  1. Plan and de-energize: Before intervening, switch off the power source.
  2. Lock out and signal: Prevent re-energization (lockout/tag) and signal the area.
  3. Verify the absence of voltage: Confirm with a detector/multimeter that the circuit is without voltage.
  4. Work protected: Use PPE, keep the area safe and follow the procedures; re-energize only at the end, with everything checked.

In practice: Before working on a panel during setup, the professional turns off the circuit breaker, locks it out with a padlock and tag, and checks with the detector that there is no voltage. This simple ritual — de-energize and verify — is what separates a safe job from a serious accident.

Summary

  • Electricity kills: shock, arc and fire demand respect and procedure.
  • De-energize, lock out, verify the absence of voltage and signal is the safe basis.
  • NR-10 governs electrical safety in Brazil and requires training of workers.
